articles.title: Nancy Cox, a CDC veteran and a stalwart in global flu research, dies at 77
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Health Nancy Cox, a CDC veteran and a stalwart in global flu research, dies at 77 Known for dedication and diplomacy, she was critical to surveillance efforts and pandemic preparedness Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Nancy Cox headed the influenza team at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention for 22 years. James Gathany/CDC By Helen Branswell April 25, 2026 Infectious Diseases Correspondent Helen Branswell Helen Branswell covers issues broadly related to infectious diseases, including outbreaks, preparedness, research, and vaccine development. Follow her on Mastodon and Bluesky . You can reach Helen on Signal at hbranswell.01. Get email alerts for Helen Branswell Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Nancy Cox, who for decades was a global leader in influenza research, has died. Cox headed the influenza team at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention for 22 years, shepherding it from a branch of 14 people to a division of over 100. She was also director of the World Health Organization’s Collaborating Center for the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and Control of Influenza at the CDC. Cox died Thursday from glioblastoma, a cancer of the brain. She was 77. Advertisement A virologist, Cox led the CDC’s efforts to respond to seasonal flu and prepare for pandemic influenza. But she was at least as well known for her work fostering global efforts to enhance monitoring for the evolution of seasonal flu viruses and spot emerging viruses with pandemic potential. James Gathany/CDC By Helen Branswell April 25, 2026 Infectious Diseases Correspondent Helen Branswell Helen Branswell covers issues broadly related to infectious diseases, including outbreaks, preparedness, research, and vaccine development. Follow her on Mastodon and Bluesky . You can reach Helen on Signal at hbranswell.01. Get email alerts for Helen Branswell Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Nancy Cox, who for decades was a global leader in influenza research, has died. Cox headed the influenza team at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention for 22 years, shepherding it from a branch of 14 people to a division of over 100. She was also director of the World Health Organization’s Collaborating Center for the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and Control of Influenza at the CDC. Cox died Thursday from glioblastoma, a cancer of the brain. She was 77. Advertisement A virologist, Cox led the CDC’s efforts to respond to seasonal flu and prepare for pandemic influenza. But she was at least as well known for her work fostering global efforts to enhance monitoring for the evolution of seasonal flu viruses and spot emerging viruses with pandemic potential.
